records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Realized (-īzd); p. pr. & vb. n. Realizing (-ī`zng).]

defs: To make real; to convert from the imaginary or fictitious into the actual; to bring into concrete existence; to effectuate; to accomplish; as, to realize a scheme or project.
quotes: We realize what Archimedes had only in hypothesis, weighing a single grain against the globe of earth.

defs: To cause to seem real; to impress upon the mind as actual; to feel vividly or strongly; to make one's own in apprehension or experience.
quotes: Many coincidences . . . soon begin to appear in them [Greek inscriptions] which realize ancient history to us.
We can not realize it in thought, that the object . . . had really no being at any past moment.

defs: To convert into real property; to make real estate of; as, to realize his fortune.
To acquire as an actual possession; to obtain as the result of plans and efforts; to gain; to get; as, to realize large profits from a speculation.
quotes: Knighthood was not beyond the reach of any man who could by diligent thrift realize a good estate.

defs: To convert into actual money; as, to realize assets.
To convert any kind of property into money, especially property representing investments, as shares in stock companies, bonds, etc.
quotes: Wary men took the alarm, and began to realize, a word now first brought into use to express the conversion of ideal property into something real.
